finding: Individuals maintain unwarranted certainty in their judgments despite being frequently wrong; confidence judgments are systematically overestimated across varied question formats.

People perceive themselves as more knowledgeable about their peers than their peers are about them; this asymmetry arises from differential weighting of observable behaviors versus private cognitions and extends to group-level perceptions.

Individuals see themselves as less conforming than their peers; this asymmetry stems from weighting introspective information more heavily when assessing self and behavioral information more heavily when assessing others.

People rely more heavily on individuating information when it is presented by a human communicator than a non-human source; conversational norms governing assumed relevance influence which information shapes judgment.

People underestimate personal risk through an illusion of control rather than unrealistic optimism; perceived control over outcomes, not generalized expectancy for positive outcomes, drives risk underestimation.

Encoding by race is reversible when coalitional cues no longer correlate with racial categories; brief exposure to an alternate social environment markedly reduces racial categorization despite lifetime experience.

Motor movement direction affects motivational orientation and preference; clockwise rotation increases preference for novelty and openness while counterclockwise rotation increases preference for familiarity.
Sex differences in jealousy reflect evolved adaptive mechanisms rather than beliefs about conditional probabilities of infidelity types; these differences replicate across cultures.
Extraneous disgust increases moral judgment severity; this effect depends on individual sensitivity to bodily sensations and differs from effects of other emotions like sadness.

Implicit activation of a social identity changes performance on quantitative tasks in the direction predicted by associated stereotypes; identity salience modulates performance via stereotype-consistent direction.

Repetition priming effects are stronger for low-frequency than high-frequency words; masking the prime eliminates this frequency attenuation, indicating two distinct components: a brief lexical effect and a long-term episodic effect.
Spacing between repetitions of items affects memory for those items; longer intervals between exposures influence retention differently than massed repetition.
People produce high rates of false recall and false recognition for non-presented associates of studied items; the act of recall enhances later remembering of both studied and non-studied material.
Free recall of lists exhibits an asymmetric serial position effect characterized by strong primacy in early positions and stronger, extended recency in later positions.
Response latencies for word-pair judgment tasks show dependencies between successive decisions; common association between word pairs facilitates responses to both words.
Exposure to objective coherence in patterns increases reported meaning in life relative to exposure to incoherence; this effect occurs independent of changes in affect.
Amygdala activation to stimulus valence depends dynamically on evaluative processing goals; negative information shows a smaller effect of goal-based modulation than positive information.

Implicit and explicit attitudes form from different information sources; explicit attitudes respond to conscious verbal information while implicit attitudes respond to subliminal primes, creating attitude conflict.

Automatic evaluation of stimuli produces immediate behavioral predispositions; positive evaluations generate approach tendencies and negative evaluations generate avoidance tendencies regardless of task relevance.
Subliminal stimulation can increase basic drive arousal but does not reliably form subliminal associations with behavioral consequences.
